does: This paper presents a method for controlling potentially unreliable or misaligned AI agents by resampling their proposed actions before execution. The core contribution is a control framework that allows supervisors to override or reject agent actions and request alternative samples from the agent's policy, enabling safety-conscious governance of autonomous systems. The approach leverages the ability to repeatedly query an agent for different action options, keeping only those that meet specified safety or performance criteria. This resampling mechanism provides a practical pathway to constrain agent behavior without requiring architectural modifications to the agent itself, making it applicable to black-box systems or third-party AI agents whose internals cannot be modified.
connects: This work builds on the broader AI safety literature addressing the alignment problem and control of potentially misaligned agents. It extends prior research on human oversight mechanisms and safe reinforcement learning by offering a simple, sampling-based alternative to more complex approaches like adversarial training, inverse reinforcement learning, or intrinsic safety constraints. The resampling strategy relates to existing work on rejection sampling and importance weighting in machine learning, as well as to recent efforts on behavioral governance of large language models and general-purpose agents. It also connects to the tradition of formal verification and runtime monitoring that seeks to ensure autonomous systems remain within safe operational bounds regardless of their initial training objectives.
enables: This framework opens pathways for practical safety enforcement in scenarios where users or operators interact with third-party or proprietary AI agents. It enables exploration of optimal resampling budgets and rejection criteria that balance safety guarantees against computational costs and latency constraints. Future work could develop theory characterizing when resampling is sufficient for control, extend the method to multi-agent settings, and integrate it with uncertainty quantification techniques to guide which actions warrant rejection. The approach also suggests research into designing agent policies that remain controllable under resampling, as well as protocols for adversarial robustness where agents cannot simply learn to game rejection criteria.
